core_behavior: The system must authenticate users quickly and efficiently, allowing them to access their accounts with minimal delay.
The system must verify user credentials against a secure database and grant access if the credentials are valid.
The system must provide feedback to the user regarding the success or failure of the login attempt.
inputs: Username: A unique identifier for the user, such as an email address or username string.
Password: A secret string associated with the username, used for authentication.
Optional: Two-factor authentication (2FA) code if 2FA is enabled for the user account.
outputs: Success message: Confirmation that the user has successfully logged in.
Failure message: Explanation of why the login attempt failed (e.g., incorrect password, account locked).
Optional: Prompt for 2FA code if 2FA is enabled and the initial credentials are correct.
assumptions: The system will use a secure hashing algorithm (e.g., bcrypt) to store and verify passwords.
The system will support two-factor authentication as an optional security feature.
The system will maintain a session for the user upon successful login, using secure cookies.
The system will be designed to handle a high volume of login requests concurrently.
failure_modes: Incorrect credentials: The system will return a failure message and allow the user to retry.
Account locked: If the account is locked due to multiple failed login attempts, the system will inform the user and provide instructions for unlocking the account.
System error: In case of a system error (e.g., database connection failure), the system will return a generic error message and log the error for further investigation.
edge_cases: Empty username or password: The system will return an error message indicating that both fields are required.
SQL injection attempts: The system will sanitize inputs to prevent SQL injection attacks.
Brute force attacks: The system will implement rate limiting and account lockout mechanisms after a certain number of failed login attempts.
security_constraints: The system must use HTTPS to encrypt data in transit.
Passwords must be stored using a secure hashing algorithm with a unique salt for each password.
The system must implement measures to prevent common security threats such as SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), and cross-site request forgery (CSRF).
The system must log all login attempts, successful or failed, for auditing purposes.
performance_constraints: The system must authenticate users within 200 milliseconds under normal load conditions.
The system must be able to handle at least 10,000 concurrent login requests without significant degradation in performance.
The system must be scalable to accommodate increased load as the user base grows.
